perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, no earlier than 18 months after the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return timeframe to 'no earlier than 18 months' after termination introduces a delay that could violate data protection regulations that require prompt return or destruction of data upon request or termination of the agreement. Some regulations require a shorter timeframe.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the GDPR grants data subjects the right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'). The modified text's delay in returning or destroying data directly contradicts the GDPR's requirement to erase personal data without undue delay when it is no longer necessary for the purposes for which it was collected or processed, or when the data subject withdraws consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a delay of 18 months before the recipient is obligated to return or destroy confidential information. This directly contradicts Article 17 of the GDPR, which requires erasure of personal data 'without undue delay' when it is no longer necessary. The scraped law snippet explicitly states that 'the data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies'. The 18-month delay is inconsistent with the GDPR's mandate of erasing data 'without undue delay'.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years] term. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during the term shall remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of 1 year. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during the term shall terminate completely 1 year after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality obligation to only one year post-termination is a legal contradiction because it might conflict with trade secret laws, which often protect trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets indefinitely, as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. The changed text's one-year limit on confidentiality contradicts this, as it would allow the recipient to disclose trade secrets after that period, even if they still qualify for protection under the DTSA.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by limiting the confidentiality obligation to one year post-termination, which conflicts with the general principle of trade secret law that protects trade secrets indefinitely, as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. The original text allowed for indefinite confidentiality, which aligns with trade secret protection. The modified text undermines this protection by imposing a time limit, thus potentially allowing for the disclosure of trade secrets after one year, even if they still qualify for protection under laws such as the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as mentioned in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 18 USC 1836, which pertains to civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets, indicating a legal framework for their protection.

perturbation.original_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that in the event of a breach of this Agreement, the Discloser's sole remedy will be a fixed penalty of 1000 EUR, waiving all rights to seek injunctive relief or actual and exemplary damages.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the discloser's remedy to a fixed penalty and waiving the right to injunctive relief can be problematic if the confidential information involves trade secrets. This limitation may not be enforceable depending on the applicable jurisdiction and the nature of the confidential information because it prevents the Discloser from being properly compensated.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), adopted by most US states, allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets and damages to compensate for the loss. The changed text, by waiving injunctive relief and limiting damages, directly contradicts the remedies available under the UTSA for trade secret misappropriation, making it potentially unenforceable in many jurisdictions.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Discloser to seek injunctive relief and damages in case of a breach, while the changed text limits the remedy to a fixed penalty, waiving rights to injunctive relief and actual/exemplary damages. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA allows for injunctive relief and damages for trade secret misappropriation, and the changed text directly contradicts this. While the provided snippets are not directly helpful, the Law Explanation clearly indicates the contradiction with the UTSA. The changed text removes the right to injunctive relief and limits damages, thus creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Confidential Information shall be identified either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the Recipient of the confidential nature of the information. Such notification shall be done orally, by e-mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Confidential Information shall only be identified by marking it as such in the case of written materials. Orally disclosed information will not be considered confidential under any circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This modification requires that confidential information is only protected if physically marked. This contradicts established legal principles, particularly concerning trade secrets, where oral disclosures, if properly documented or understood within a confidential relationship, can be protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition § 39 defines trade secret and does not mandate that information must be marked as confidential to qualify for trade secret protection. The modification's insistence on marking written materials and exclusion of oral disclosures contradicts the Restatement's more flexible approach, potentially leading to the loss of trade secret protection for validly disclosed confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that orally disclosed information will not be considered confidential, which contradicts the principle that oral disclosures can be protected under trade secret law if properly documented or understood within a confidential relationship. This is highlighted in the perturbation explanation, which states that the change contradicts legal principles, particularly concerning trade secrets, where oral disclosures can be protected.
